metadata.summary: Spring Boot 3.x layered architecture: controller → service → repository with DTOs, JPA entities, @Transactional boundaries, and ProblemDetail error handling.

content.title: Spring Boot Patterns
content.summary: Spring Boot 3.x layered architecture with controller → service → repository pattern, DTOs separate from JPA entities, Lombok for immutability, MapStruct for mapping, Bean Validation for inputs, ProblemDetail for error responses, and @Transactional boundaries on service operations.

why.p: Spring Boot 3 changed namespaces from javax.* to jakarta.* and LLMs trained on older corpora mix them. The most frequent agent failures are anemic domain models (all logic in services, entities as data bags), N+1 queries from missing JOIN FETCH or @EntityGraph, and @Transactional on private methods or self-invocation which silently does nothing. ArchUnit and Testcontainers enforce layering and catch N+1 in CI.

list.item: Bootstrapping or extending a Spring Boot 3.x service with the canonical Controller → Service → Repository layering, DTOs separate from JPA entities, and @Transactional boundaries on the service layer.
Generating new resource endpoints (REST or gRPC) that follow existing project conventions: Lombok, MapStruct, Bean Validation, ProblemDetail error handling.
Refactoring legacy Spring MVC code toward constructor injection, immutable DTOs, and Pageable/Page<T> paging.
Adding cross-cutting concerns via filters, interceptors, or @RestControllerAdvice rather than duplicating logic.
The team chose Quarkus, Micronaut, or Helidon — Spring's reflection-heavy startup and AOT story differs; don't backport patterns.
Pure event-driven workers without HTTP — use Spring Modulith or plain @KafkaListener/@RabbitListener services; the controller layer is dead weight.
Reactive stack (spring-webflux) — most templates here assume blocking servlet stack; Mono/Flux plumbing is different.
Native image (GraalVM) without explicit reachability config — Lombok + reflection-based Jackson causes hidden failures at runtime.

section.name: controller-layer
section.p: Controllers receive HTTP requests, delegate to services, and return HTTP responses. They must never contain business logic and must not return JPA entities directly.

rule.statement: Controllers use @RestController, @RequestMapping, @GetMapping, @PostMapping, @PutMapping, @DeleteMapping. Inject services via constructor and @RequiredArgsConstructor. Use @Valid on @RequestBody to trigger Bean Validation. Return ResponseEntity or a DTO mapped by a MapStruct mapper. Never return JPA entities.
rule.rationale: Returning entities exposes lazy-loaded proxy objects to Jackson serialization, which can trigger LazyInitializationException after the transaction ends or leak internal fields. DTOs are the contract boundary.

example.title: Clean controller with validation and mapping
example.note: Constructor injection, @Valid on requests, mapping service responses through DTOs, appropriate HTTP status codes.

antipattern.description: Controllers placing business logic directly in the handler method or returning JPA entities.
antipattern.reason: Business logic in controllers violates separation of concerns and makes unit testing harder. Returning entities causes LazyInitializationException and leaks internal state.

section.name: service-layer
section.p: Services orchestrate repository calls, apply business rules, and wrap writes in transactions. They are the enforcement point for transactional boundaries.

rule.statement: Service classes use @Service and constructor injection with @RequiredArgsConstructor. Annotate the class with @Transactional(readOnly = true) as the default; override with @Transactional (no readOnly) on individual write methods. Never apply @Transactional to private methods or call them directly on the same bean.
rule.rationale: @Transactional on private methods is silently ignored — Spring AOP proxies intercept only public method calls from outside the bean. Self-invocation (this.writeMethod()) bypasses the proxy and runs without a transaction.

example.title: Service with transactional defaults
example.note: Class-level @Transactional(readOnly = true) with method-level @Transactional override on writes. Constructor injection for all dependencies.

antipattern.description: Placing business logic in controllers instead of services or calling private transactional methods via this.
antipattern.reason: Services are the proper encapsulation layer for business rules and transactional boundaries. Private method calls bypass the proxy.

section.name: repository-and-jpa
section.p: Repositories extend JpaRepository and provide data access, including support for dynamic queries via Specifications. Entities are persistence objects; DTOs are transfer objects.

rule.statement: Repositories extend JpaRepository<Entity, ID>. Use JpaSpecificationExecutor for dynamic queries. Never build JPQL strings by concatenation. DTOs are separate classes, never the same as entities. Always use @Column(nullable = false, updatable = false) for immutable audit fields like createdAt.
rule.rationale: String concatenation in JPQL opens SQL injection vectors and is untyped. Specifications are type-safe, composable, and testable independently. Separating DTOs from entities enforces boundaries and allows independent evolution.

example.title: JPA entity with audit fields and repository
example.note: Audit fields with @CreationTimestamp/@UpdateTimestamp, @Version for optimistic locking, repository interface with Specifications.

section.name: dto-and-mapping
section.p: DTOs (Data Transfer Objects) separate the API contract from the internal model. MapStruct mappers convert between entities and DTOs.

rule.statement: Request and response DTOs are Java records (Java 17+) or immutable classes. Never use @Data on DTOs or expose mutable fields. Use MapStruct with componentModel = SPRING for automatic bean-based mapping. Ensure MapStruct annotation processing is wired in pom.xml or build.gradle.
rule.rationale: Records eliminate boilerplate and are immutable by construction. Missing MapStruct annotation processing causes the mapper bean to be missing at startup, a frequent agent error that only surfaces when Spring fails to autowire the mapper.

example.title: Record DTOs and MapStruct mapper
example.note: Records for DTOs, MapStruct mapper with componentModel = SPRING, separate request and response types.

antipattern.description: Using the same class for both entity and DTO or passing raw entities through API boundaries.
antipattern.reason: Entities should never be exposed over HTTP. The API contract (DTO) must be independent of the internal persistence model.

section.name: exception-handling
section.p: Global exception handling via @RestControllerAdvice translates application exceptions to HTTP responses using RFC 7807 ProblemDetail.

rule.statement: Define a @RestControllerAdvice class that handles all exception types and returns ProblemDetail. Never return custom JSON error shapes; ProblemDetail is the standard RFC 7807 format. Map application exceptions to appropriate HTTP status codes (404 for not found, 400 for validation, 409 for conflict, 500 for internal errors).
rule.rationale: ProblemDetail is Spring Boot 3's built-in RFC 7807 implementation. Using custom error shapes forces clients to maintain two parsers. Standard formats allow generic error handling on the client.

example.title: Global exception handler with ProblemDetail
example.note: @RestControllerAdvice with method handlers per exception type, all returning ProblemDetail.

antipattern.description: Returning custom JSON shapes for errors or allowing exceptions to propagate uncaught to the client.
antipattern.reason: Custom error shapes fragment the API contract. Uncaught exceptions expose stack traces and internal details. RFC 7807 ProblemDetail is the standardized format.

section.name: bean-validation-and-jakarta-namespace
section.p: Spring Boot 3 uses jakarta.* packages exclusively. Bean Validation annotations enforce input constraints before business logic executes.

rule.statement: Use jakarta.validation.* constraints (@NotBlank, @Email, @Size, @Pattern, @Valid) on request DTOs. Never mix javax.* and jakarta.* imports in the same file. Spring Boot 3 depends on jakarta.* exclusively. Always run a namespace check as part of the CI gate.
rule.rationale: LLMs trained on Spring Boot 2.x examples emit javax.* imports. These fail to compile with Spring Boot 3 dependencies. Mixing namespaces produces cryptic errors.

section.name: n-plus-one-prevention
section.p: The most common runtime failure in Spring Boot + Hibernate applications is N+1 queries: accessing a lazy-loaded relationship inside a loop issues one query per iteration plus one initial query.

rule.statement: Use @Query with JOIN FETCH or @EntityGraph on repository methods to eagerly load relationships. Test assertions must verify query counts via Hibernate Statistics. Never access a lazy relationship inside a controller or service loop without prior eager loading.
rule.rationale: Hibernate's lazy loading is transparent but expensive at scale. One loop over N users accessing a lazy organization relationship issues N+1 queries. Eager loading at the repository boundary prevents this pattern entirely.

example.title: Preventing N+1 with JOIN FETCH
example.note: JOIN FETCH loads the relationship in the same query; @EntityGraph is an annotation-based alternative.

antipattern.description: Iterating over a collection and accessing lazy relationships: for (User u : users) { System.out.println(u.getOrganization().getName()); }
antipattern.reason: Each getOrganization() triggers a SELECT. For N users, N+1 queries are issued (one to fetch users, N to fetch each organization).
